did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with three hidden layers and optimizers (SGD, RMSprop, Adam, Nadam) to automatically classify skin cancer and benign tumor lesions (dermatofibroma, nevus pigmentosus, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma) from the ISIC dataset, achieving 99% accuracy with the Adam optimizer.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for automatic skin cancer classification. It details the methodology (CNN architecture, optimizers, ISIC dataset), describes the system design and training process (3000 training images, 100 epochs), and reports specific quantitative results (99% accuracy with Adam optimizer). Although published in a conference series (IOP Conf. Ser.: Mater. Sci. Eng.), it contains new empirical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for Automatic Skin Cancer Classification System
journal: IOP Conference Series: Materials Science and Engineering
brief_summary: This study developed an automatic skin cancer classification system using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to distinguish between benign tumors and malignant lesions. The model, consisting of three hidden layers with 3x3 filters and output channels of 16, 32, and 64, was trained on an augmented ISIC dataset containing 4000 images across four classes: dermatofibroma, nevus pigmentosus, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma. The system was evaluated using SGD, RMSprop, Adam, and Nadam optimizers with a learning rate of 0.001 over 100 epochs. The Adam optimizer achieved the best performance, yielding 99% accuracy and a loss of 0.0346 on the validation set. The results indicate that the proposed CNN model effectively classifies skin lesions with high precision, recall, and F1-scores, outperforming existing systems.

lesion_types_studied.details: Benign tumor class used in the ISIC dataset for classification; 1000 images in augmented dataset, 257 in validation set.
Benign tumor class (originating from melanocytes) used in the ISIC dataset for classification; 1000 images in augmented dataset, 246 in validation set.
Malignant skin cancer class used in the ISIC dataset for classification; 1000 images in augmented dataset, 255 in validation set.
Malignant skin cancer class used in the ISIC dataset for classification; 1000 images in augmented dataset, 242 in validation set.
Mentioned in the Introduction as the most common skin cancer in Indonesia (65.5%) and in references, but not included in the experimental classification task or dataset.

all_task_types.target: Four discrete classes: dermatofibroma, nevus pigmentosus, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma.
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a multi-class classification task using a CNN with softmax activation to identify skin lesions from the ISIC dataset into 4 classes. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign tumors vs. malignant skin cancers) rather than continuous values. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-scores.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset (Augmented)
datasets_used.description: An augmented version of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset containing 4000 dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The dataset was created by augmenting the original ISIC data to balance the classes.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model (3000 images, 75% of the augmented dataset).
use.validation: Used for validation and performance evaluation (1000 images, 25% of the augmented dataset).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, loss, precision, recall, and F1-scores on a validation set, with comparative statements regarding optimizer performance (e.g., Adam outperforming SGD, RMSprop, and Nadam) but without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Dataset (Augmented).experiment: CNN with various optimizers (SGD, RMSprop, Adam, Nadam) over 100 epochs

ISIC Dataset (Augmented).notes: The Adam optimizer provided the best performance compared to SGD, RMSprop, and Nadam. The model did not show signs of overfitting as the difference between training and validation accuracy was small, and validation loss continued to decrease. The system achieved high precision, recall, and F1-scores (close to 1).

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation of the ISIC dataset to balance classes and increase data volume (4000 images total, 1000 per class)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The original ISIC dataset was augmented to create a balanced dataset containing 4000 images total, with 1000 images per class (dermatofibroma, nevus pigmentosus, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma). This augmentation was performed to increase the amount of data and improve the performance of the skin cancer detection system.

conclusions: The proposed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model effectively classifies skin lesions into four classes (dermatofibroma, nevus pigmentosus, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma) with high performance.
The Adam optimizer provided the best performance among the tested optimizers (SGD, RMSprop, Adam, Nadam), achieving an accuracy of 99% and a loss of 0.0346 on the validation set.
The system achieved high precision, recall, and F1-scores (close to 1.00) for dermatofibroma and squamous cell carcinoma, and 0.98 for nevus pigmentosus and melanoma.
The results obtained by the proposed CNN model outperform the performance of existing skin cancer classification systems mentioned in the literature review.
The model did not show signs of overfitting, as the difference between training and validation accuracy was small and validation loss continued to decrease.
Other optimizers (SGD, RMSprop, Nadam) exhibited performance instability, characterized by sudden decreases in accuracy and rises in loss at certain epochs.
important_remarks: The study uses a highly artificial, perfectly balanced augmented dataset (1000 images per class) derived from the ISIC archive, which does not reflect the natural class imbalance found in clinical practice.

The dataset is small (4000 total images) and lacks an independent external test set; evaluation was performed solely on a 25% validation split of the augmented data, raising concerns about data leakage and generalizability.
The model excludes Basal Cell Carcinoma, the most common skin cancer in Indonesia (65.5% per introduction), limiting the clinical relevance of the system for comprehensive skin cancer screening.

The reported 99% accuracy is likely inflated due to the use of data augmentation to balance classes rather than real-world diverse imaging conditions, and no statistical significance testing was performed.
The paper claims the system is 'promising' for medical personnel but provides no evidence of clinical workflow integration, user study, or prospective validation.
Other optimizers (SGD, RMSprop, Nadam) showed instability with sudden drops in accuracy and spikes in loss, suggesting the model's performance is sensitive to optimizer choice and training dynamics.
The CNN architecture is relatively simple (3 hidden layers, 89,124 parameters) compared to state-of-the-art deep learning models, potentially limiting its ability to capture complex lesion features.
